Row 2: X □ X □ X (starts with X, alternates)
Row 3: □ X □ X □ (starts with □, alternates)
Row 4: X □ X □ X (starts with X, alternates)
Row 5: □ X □ X □ (starts with □, alternates)

Yes, this is a perfect checkerboard pattern where:
- Odd rows start with □
- Even rows start with X

Distractor grid 1:
□ X X □ X
X □ X X □

Distractor grid 2:
□ □ X X □

□ X X □ X

□ X X □ □

This is also NOT a checkerboard. There are many breaks:
- Row 1, position 1-2: □ □ (two empty adjacent)
- Row 1, position 3-4: X X (two Xs adjacent)
- And many more...

So the key distinguishing feature of the target grid is that it forms a perfect checkerboard pattern where no two cells of the same type are adjacent (horizontally or vertically).


The target grid maintains strict alternation both across rows and down columns, creating a consistent pattern where every cell differs from its immediate neighbors.
